coredata.dc:title: Cellulose-based photo-enhanced persistent room-temperature phosphorescent materials by space stacking effects
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Maozhu
Li, Tang
Liu, Yingchun
Lin, Xuliang
Zu, Xihong
Mu, Yingxiao
Chen, Liheng
Huo, Yanping
Qin, Yanlin
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose is environmentally friendly bio-based polymer with a large number of hydroxyl groups in its structure, which can easily form hydrogen bond networks. It has the potential to become excellent persistent room-temperature phosphorescence (pRTP) material through rational design. In this work, we reported a simple and universal strategy to obtain polymer film by connecting aromatic derivatives onto cellulose chains and drying them by hot press process. The emission color of pRTP from blue-green to red can be tuned by benzene or different polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons as grafted groups, as well as the performance of photo-enhanced pRTP property. Notably, the lifetime of the best-performing film could be enhanced from 282.1 ms to 571.1 ms after irradiation for 1 min at room temperature. All the hot-pressed films were able to withstand Young's modulus up to nearly 18.0 GPa and tensile strength of around 80 MPa. The excellent luminescent and mechanical properties were derived from space stacking effects of the grafted groups and abundant intermolecular hydrogen bonding. These results will provide the effective design strategy for the modulation of smart-response pRTP materials, and expand the application range of cellulose-based materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Persistent room-temperature phosphorescence
Cellulose-based material
Photo-enhanced
Hydrogen bonding

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation process and (b) space stacking effects of the cellulose-based hot-pressed film. (c) Proposed mechanism for pRTP systems.
Photographs of C1-C4 turning on and off after 0 min and 1 min irradiation.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CMC and C1-C4. (b) Full scan XPS spectrum of C3. High-resolution XPS (c) C 1 s, (d) N 1 s and (e) O 1 s spectra of C3. (f) XRD pattern of C1-C4.

(a) Steady-state PL (blue line) and transient PL (delayed 8 ms) spectra of C1-C4 before (gray line) and after (orange line) photo-enhanced under ambient conditions. (b) Lifetime decay profiles of the emission band around 550 nm of C3 before and after photo-enhanced under ambient conditions. The inset showed the enlarged view of the lifetime decay profile of C3 before photo-enhanced. (c) The transient PL decay image (delayed 8 ms) of C3 after irradiated 1 min. (d) Proposed mechanism for photo-enhanced pRTP.

(a) The molecular structures of C1-C4 in free state and the calculated energy diagram. (b) The calculated ESP maps of isolated molecule and dimers based on C1-C4 in free state. (c) Possible mechanism on space stacking effects.
Demonstration of multiple data encryption using the normal pRTP compound (Normal) and C1-C4.

head.ce:title: Cellulose-based photo-enhanced persistent room-temperature phosphorescent materials by space stacking effects

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Light Industry, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose is environmentally friendly bio-based polymer with a large number of hydroxyl groups in its structure, which can easily form hydrogen bond networks. It has the potential to become excellent persistent room-temperature phosphorescence (pRTP) material through rational design. In this work, we reported a simple and universal strategy to obtain polymer film by connecting aromatic derivatives onto cellulose chains and drying them by hot press process. The emission color of pRTP from blue-green to red can be tuned by benzene or different polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons as grafted groups, as well as the performance of photo-enhanced pRTP property. Notably, the lifetime of the best-performing film could be enhanced from 282.1 ms to 571.1 ms after irradiation for 1 min at room temperature. All the hot-pressed films were able to withstand Young's modulus up to nearly 18.0 GPa and tensile strength of around 80 MPa. The excellent luminescent and mechanical properties were derived from space stacking effects of the grafted groups and abundant intermolecular hydrogen bonding. These results will provide the effective design strategy for the modulation of smart-response pRTP materials, and expand the application range of cellulose-based materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Persistent room-temperature phosphorescence (pRTP) materials have been widely used in optical display, information encryption, medical imaging and other fields, and have attracted extensive attention in recent years [1,2]. With the rapid development of optical display and flexible sensor, the traditional inorganic or metal–organic complex luminescent powder is limited due to high cost and harsh preparation conditions [3,4]. Pure organic luminescent materials have the characteristics of good processability, good biocompatibility, abundant sources and low cost, which make them more widely used [5-10]. In recent years, several design strategies to suppress non-radiative transitions through intermolecular interactions, such as crystallization induction, electron coupling, carbon point, host–guest doping and organic frameworks [11-20], have been proposed to obtain high performance organic pRTP materials. Among them, single-component crystal-induced phosphorescence system is considered as the effective method to construct pRTP materials, which can not only inhibit non-radiative transitions from the rigidity of the system, but also block the triplet excitons that are easily quenched by water and oxygen molecules to a certain extent [21,22]. However, in the amorphous state of such materials, pRTP will weaken or even disappear, seriously affecting its application in practical. To overcome these problems, it is urgent to develop amorphous pRTP materials [23,24]. Polymers can create a rigid environment through strong intermolecular interactions, and are ideal candidate materials for their strong film forming ability and stability [25-27]. The relatively simple processing process makes them have the potential for commercial application. However, it is still difficult to achieve a wide range of color tunability using simple strategies. In addition, the vast majority of pRTP materials are only static pRTP observed under transient irradiation [6,28,29]. If the lifetime of the afterglow could be changed with the irradiation time to obtain dynamic pRTP, abundant encryption modes would be obtained [30-33]. Therefore, it is very challenging to select a suitable polymer framework to develop single-component polymers with dynamic pRTP properties.

The aromatic amines were grafted into the chain of cellulose through amidation reaction (more details in Supporting Information). Among it, aniline, 1-naphthylamine, 9-aminophenanthrene and 1-aminopyrene groups were selected as the chromophore, which were introduced to synthesize C1, C2, C3 and C4 with photo-enhanced pRTP properties. The structure properties of them were identified by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorption characteristic peak at 1640 cm−1 represented the elongation of CONH units, which proved the grafted of aromatic amines. Meanwhile, C-N stretching was observed at 1315 cm−1, confirming the preparation of the polymer. To explore the types of bonds in the cellulose-based polymers, the XPS results displayed the surface groups of C1-C4 (Fig. 3b-e and S1-S3). The full-scan XPS spectra of C1-C4 displayed the presence of C, N and O elements. For the high-resolution spectra of C1-C4, the C 1 s band was deconvolved into three similar peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.6 eV, and 287.8 eV, corresponding to C–C, C-O/C-N, and C = O, respectively. N 1 s analysis showed the presence of C-N (399.6 eV) and N–H (401.3 eV). The O 1 s bands of C = O and C-O have two peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.6 eV, respectively [49]. The above analysis revealed that the grafted groups bond in a similar way among C1-C4, which the amino group on the aromatic amine bonds with the carboxymethyl group. In addition, powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) has been used to characterize the microstructure of hot-pressed films prepared from C1-C4 in Fig. 3f. The results depicted broad dispersion peaks, indicating that the films of C1-C4 were amorphous state, which expect them facilitate for processability and practical application.

To investigate the thermal stability of C1-C4, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was examined in Fig. S4. The thermal decomposition temperatures (Td, 5% weight loss) were recorded to be nearly 270 °C for C1-C4. When the final combustion reaches 800℃, 20% of their weight remains, which is nitrogenous carbon black left after the combustion of aromatic compounds. All of them showed great thermal stability, which can be hot press processing below 100 ℃ and general usage at room temperature.

The tensile strength measurements were performed to evaluate the mechanical properties of the hot-pressed films (C1-C4). And the corresponding tensile results (tensile strength, strain to failure and Young's modulus) were presented in Fig. S5 and Table S1. All the hot-pressed films exhibited tensile strength around 80 MPa and Young's modulus up to nearly 18.0 GPa. Moreover, the average strain to failure of them was in the range 5–8 %. Such high strength benefitted from the abundant intermolecular hydrogen bonds in cellulose and the hot pressing process which could close the distance among molecules.

As depicted in Figs. 2 and 4 a, all films appeared blue emission bands around 410 and 430 nm with the photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) of 5–8 % under UV lamp. However, different afterglow could be visualized by the naked eye after closing the UV lamp. Among them, C2 and C3 showed persistent luminescence which lasted for several seconds after UV lamp off. By comparison, C1 and C4 had no significant afterglow, but weak decay photoluminescence (PL) signals could be detected. Interestingly, we observed dramatic improvement in the brightness and duration of C1-C4 afterglow after 1 min of continuous UV light irradiated and then turning off the lamp. Correspondingly, their lifetime also increased significantly after UV irradiation in Fig. 4b and Fig. S6, and Table S2. It is worth noting that the photo-enhanced effect is most pronounced for C3, whose lifetime at 530 nm boosted from 282.1 ms to 571.1 ms. From the transient PL decay image (delayed 8 ms) of C3 after irradiated 1 min in Fig. 4c, the color change from red to blue indicating the attenuation of the emission intensity without wavelength shift. This phenomenon of photo-enhanced pRTP is rarely in non-doped polymers, and even less reported in intrinsic bio-based polymers.

To explore the source of this photo-enhanced pRTP phenomenon, we selected C3 as a model for discussion. As the irradiation time increases from 0 to 1 min, the pRTP intensity of C3 was gradually raised (Fig. S7). When C3 was placed under ambient conditions for about 10 min, the photo-enhanced pRTP gradually disappeared, indicating that the process was reversible. Placing C3 in liquid nitrogen, it was achieved a strong afterglow without the process of photo-enhanced, even higher than the effect of photo-enhanced at room temperature (Fig. S8). This suggested that the prolonged lifetime may be due to the suppression of molecular vibration at low temperature, thus reducing the non-radiative transition procedure. Therefore, we speculated that the process of photo-enhanced may played a similar role in reducing non-radiative transitions at low temperature. Generally, materials with triplet states are very sensitive to oxygen, since the triplet excitons will be quenched by the collision with triplet ground state of oxygen molecules [21]. Thus, we will explore the phosphorescent decay process of C3 in vacuum. Surprisingly, the strong afterglow could be observed in vacuum without UV lamp irradiation for 1 min, and the emission intensity is slightly higher than that of photo-enhanced under air (Fig. S8). Based on the above results, the possible mechanism of photo-enhanced pRTP effect could be proposed as shown in Fig. 4d. The triplet excitons were generated from the singlet one through intersystem crossing (ISC) process. When the films contacted with oxygen under ambient conditions, part of the triplet excitons in materials were quenched by the interaction with triplet oxygen. Consequently, only weak persistent luminescence occurred when the UV lamp switched on and off instantaneously. After UV irradiation 1 min, the apparent afterglow with longer lifetime could be fully observed when triplet oxygen was excited and consumed.

In order to analyze the molecular energy levels and the process of pRTP production, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using B3LYP/6-31G(d) in the gas phase at the repeating unit of C1-C4 to calculate the HOMO/LUMO distribution (Fig. 5 a). Due to the semi-rigid of the main chain molecule, the HOMOs and LUMOs of the molecule were distributed on the grafted group, indicating that the phosphorescent chromophore is distributed on the grafted group. The aggregation of grafted groups forming emission centers, which are then confined by intermolecular hydrogen bonds, is the key to persistent luminescence. In addition, the difference in electron density distribution can be reflected by calculation of electrostatic potential (ESP) (Fig. 5b). The negative electron region is mainly located in the region of the grafted groups, indicating that it is electron-rich, which leads to the special stacking pattern between molecular chains to a certain extent. Therefore, the π-π interactions could be promoted by the stacking of grafted groups, which inhibited the non-radiative transitions and adjusted the emission of phosphorescence.

It is reasonable that the tunable emission of C1-C4 was mainly due to the different space stacking of the grafted aromatic compounds and the π-π interaction on the aromatic luminescent groups (Fig. 5c). The cellulose matrix provides a rigid environment through multiple intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds, which reduced polymeric segmental relaxation and promoted the excitons intermigration process. Cellulose-based pRTP materials might form different intermolecular interactions, thus producing different color of afterglow in the case of structural changes of grafted group. Among them, hydrogen bonds between adjacent amides, which are the main driving forces, and the folding of cellulose chains lead to the aggregation of luminescent groups, as well as π-π interactions between grafted groups. Furthermore, the spatial stacking between the grafted group had been affected by adjusting the size of the grafted group, and then the electrons transfer process of intermolecular and intramolecular was changed.

To further explore the application of C1-C4, we designed a series of experiments to verify the multi-field application prospects. The multiple data encryption could be realized by using C1-C4 together with common pRTP luminophore. As shown in Fig. 6 , the character code “GODUT” was assembled using “G” composed of normal pRTP compound and “ODUT” made of C1-C4, respectively. Under UV excitation, the blue “GODUT” pattern can be observed clearly. When the UV lamp suddenly turns on and off, only “G” with yellow persistent luminescence occurred as static pRTP. After UV irradiation for over 1 min, the updated pattern of “GDUT” could be direct-viewing due to the dynamic pRTP properties. To our knowledge, this is the first report of dynamic pRTP properties of intrinsic bio-based polymers, which have multiple levels of data encryption and anti-counterfeiting functions, and could be utilized in practical applications. Notably, such film owns transparent and flexible features allowing it to bend or fold freely. Based on the excellent flexibility and luminescent properties of C3 film, a windmill with simple structure and a dog with relatively complex structure were fabricated (Fig. S9). Obvious pRTP effect can still be watched after irradiation 1 min, indicating that bending and folding would not affect the luminescent performance.

In summary, we developed a general and simple strategy to effectively construct color-tunable photo-enhanced pRTP cellulose-based polymer by various bulky grafted groups onto cellulose chains, using space stacking effects. The different stacking areas between aromatic amines could effectively influence the electron transfer during the pRTP process, resulting in a tunable blue-green to red afterglow. In addition, photo-enhanced pRTP performance under ambient conditions could be used for information encryption. The excellent mechanical properties of hot-pressed cellulose films will play the important role in practical applications, notably where high-strength but lightweight materials are required. Using the excellent properties of cellulose to improve the luminescent properties of materials is prospective, which has important implications for the large-scale development of low-cost and sustainable advanced optical materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22078069, 22178069, 51903254), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019B151502038, 2021A1515110409), Science and Technology Planning of Guangdong Province (No. 2019A050510042).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.136935.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
